sections.heading: 1. Favor Immutability
sections.content: Always start with val. Change it to var only when necessary. Prefer listOf() over mutableListOf() unless you truly need mutation. This prevents accidental state changes.

sections.heading: 2. Use Expression Bodies
sections.content: If a function can be written in a single expression, remove the block body and return keyword. This reduces visual noise.

sections.heading: Expression Body Example
sections.content: Comparison between non-idiomatic and idiomatic function declaration.

sections.heading: 3. Leverage String Templates
sections.content: Avoid using + for string concatenation. Use Kotlin string templates for better readability and performance.

sections.heading: String Template Example
sections.content: Replacing string concatenation with string templates.

sections.heading: 4. Master Scope Functions
sections.content: Use apply for object configuration and let for null-safety transformations. This improves clarity and intent.

sections.heading: Apply Example
sections.content: Using apply for clean UI configuration.

sections.heading: 5. Avoid !! (Not-Null Assertion)
sections.content: The !! operator can crash your app. Prefer safe calls (?.), the Elvis operator (?:), or explicit null checks for safer code.

sections.heading: 6. Meaningful Named Arguments
sections.content: When a function has multiple parameters, especially Booleans, use named arguments to make the call self-documenting.

sections.heading: Named Arguments Example
7. Sealed Classes for UI States
sections.content: Avoid multiple Boolean flags like isLoading and isError. Use a sealed class to represent UI states and create a single source of truth.

sections.heading: 8. Extension Functions for Utility
sections.content: Instead of utility classes like StringUtils, use extension functions to make code more natural and expressive.

sections.heading: Extension Property Example
sections.content: Adds email validation directly to String.

sections.heading: 9. Trailing Commas
sections.content: Using trailing commas makes Git diffs cleaner and reduces unnecessary changes when adding new items.

sections.heading: Summary Checklist
items: Can I use a string template instead of +?
Is my UI state represented by a sealed class?
Am I using Dispatchers.IO for background work?
